ECSS-E-ST-10-03C, SPACE ENGINEERING: ... branch (ECSS&#8208;E&#8208;10) this standard aims at a consistent application of on ground testing requirements to allow proper qualification and acceptance of space products Experience has demonstrated that incomplete or improper ...
ECSS-E-ST-10-03C, SPACE ENGINEERING: TESTING (01-JUN-2012)., The requirements on the systems engineering process are gathered in ECSS‐EST‐ 10; while specific aspects are further elaborated in dedicated standards, in particular: ECSS‐E‐ST‐10‐06, ECSS‐E‐ST‐10‐02 and the present standard (ECSSE‐ ST‐10‐03) In the System Engineering branch (ECSS‐E‐10) this standard aims at a consistent application of on ground testing requirements to allow proper qualification and acceptance of space products Experience has demonstrated that incomplete or improper on ground testing approach significantly increase project risks leading to late discovery of design or workmanship problem(s) or in‐orbit failure(s).
Testing is part of the system engineering process as defined in ECSS‐E‐ST‐10. This starts at the early phase of the mission when defining verification process in terms of the model philosophy and test sequence and ends at the last testing phase prior launch.
Clauses 5 and 6 are organised as follows: * general requirements for the products under test applicable to all models (clause 5.1 or 6.1); * requirements applicable to qualification model (clause 5.2 or 6.2); * requirements applicable to acceptance model (clause 5.3 or 6.3); * requirements applicable to protoflight model (clause 5.4 or 6.4); * detailed implementation requirements (clause 5.5 or 6.5); In the clause providing requirements for each model (i.e. clauses 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 6.2, 6.3 and 6.4), the first table of the clause: * lists all types of test and defines their applicability and con

results.title: ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Rev.1 – Testing (31 May 2022) | European Cooperation for Space Standardization
results.description: This standard addresses the requirements for performing verification by testing of space segment elements and space segment equipment on ground prior to launch. The document is applicable for tests performed on qualification models, flight models (tested at acceptance level) and protoflight models.
results.content:
This standard addresses the requirements for performing verification by testing of space segment elements and space segment equipment on ground prior to launch. The document is applicable for tests performed on qualification models, flight models (tested at acceptance level) and protoflight models.
General requirements for development tests pertinent to the start of the qualification test programme, NOTE Development tests are specific and are addressed in various engineering discipline standards. Content of the necessary documentation for testing activities (e.g. DRD). Due to the specific aspects of the following types of test, this Standard does not address: Space system testing (i.e.
Testing of space segment subsystems, NOTE Tests of space segment subsystems are often limited to functional tests that, in some case, are run on dedicated models. If relevant, qualification tests for space segment subsystems are assumed to be covered in the relevant discipline standards.
This standard may be tailored for the specific characteristic and constrains of a space project in conformance with ECSS-S-ST-00.

This Standard is one of the series of ECSS Standards intended to be applied together for the management, engineering, product assurance and sustainability in space projects and applications.
NOTE: Only the modified parts of the Standard are subject to the Public Review.
For the purposes of this Standard the following abbreviated terms apply.

results.title: ECSS-E-ST-10-03C Aerospace Engineering - Tests
results.description: The ECSS-E-ST-10-03C standard, ... (ECSS) organization, describes the requirements to perform verification by ground testing of space segment elements and space segment equipment before launch....
results.content:
The ECSS-E-ST-10-03C standard, ... (ECSS) organization, describes the requirements to perform verification by ground testing of space segment elements and space segment equipment before launch....
The ECSS-E-ST-10-03C standard, published by the European cooperation for space standardization (ECSS) organization, describes the requirements to perform verification by ground testing of space segment elements and space segment equipment before launch.
The ECSS-E-ST-10-03C standard is adapted for certain characteristics and constraints of a space project that complies with the ECSS-S-ST-00 standard.
Testing of equipment below space segment equipment levels (including assembly, parts and components) Testing of stand-alone software (for verification of flight or ground software, ECSS-E-ST-40 and ECSS-Q-ST-80 standards apply)
Space system testing (ie testing on space segment element), especially system validation testing

items.question: What are the specific requirements for thermal cycling tests, and how do they differ based on equipment environments?
items.answer: Thermal cycling tests require specific conditions based on the equipment's operational environment. For non-vacuum environments, ambient cycling tests are applicable, whereas vacuum tests are necessary for equipment operating in space . Depressurization failure must be considered, and pressure values are mission-specific . These tests aim to ensure equipment reliability under temperature variations .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: What are the considerations and tests necessary for re-flying space segment equipment according to the standards?
items.answer: Re-flying space segment equipment requires a comprehensive set of considerations and tests. The equipment must be re-tested according to the verification program for the new mission, ensuring readiness in accordance with the acceptance test criteria . High-level functional testing is preferred over individual low-level tests . The qualification test duration and acoustic test duration must be adjusted by adding 50 seconds for each additional flight to the standard duration . Environmental tests, including thermal vacuum and cycling, are essential . Functional and performance tests should be conducted at the beginning and end of the test program, with any anomalies evaluated for trends indicating potential issues . Testing must also include mechanical tests such as random vibration, sinusoidal vibration, acceleration, and shock tests to confirm structural integrity under flight conditions . Continuous monitoring and additional tests may be conducted to ensure electromagnetic compatibility and structural integrity, with specific tests required depending on the equipment's operational environment and configuration .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: What are the critical factors considered during the test specification definition for thermal tests, and how do they ensure testing efficacy?
items.answer: Critical factors considered during the test specification definition for thermal tests include defining the purpose and objective of the test, test requirements to be verified, and the test approach . These specifications ensure testing efficacy by listing pass/fail criteria, providing traceability for requirements, and detailing the test set-up, conditions, sequence, and facility requirements, including instrumentation and measurement accuracy . Additional elements such as test configuration, cycles, extreme temperatures, stability criteria, and functional tests at hot and cold temperature plateaux are stipulated to simulate real conditions effectively and validate equipment performance under thermal stress . The documentation requirements ensure comprehensive recording of procedures, roles, and conditions to maintain consistency and accuracy during the tests ."}
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: How does post-landing testing contribute to the resumption of space segment operations, and what components are prioritized?
items.answer: Post-landing testing is crucial for resuming space segment operations as it verifies the readiness and integrity of space segment elements after their return to Earth. This testing ensures that any equipment to be re-flown meets acceptance criteria and is free from defects or degradation from previous missions . The testing sequence includes functional and performance tests to examine the equipment's operational modes and integrity . Prioritized components for testing include the overall functional tests, rotating parts, power consumption, and propulsion systems, ensuring they operate as expected under both ambient and specific environmental conditions . These tests are designed to detect any anomalies and assure the equipment can withstand future mission requirements .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: Why is it necessary to monitor RF output for corona effects during testing, and which equipment is most affected?
items.answer: Monitoring RF output for corona effects during testing is vital for equipment critical to corona effects, ensuring that corona-induced anomalies are detected and mitigated . RF equipment susceptible to corona effects is primarily affected, especially during chamber pressure reductions, where ambient pressure can be less than Earth's atmospheric pressure causing ionization challenges .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: In what circumstances might acoustic testing be replaced by random vibration tests, and what does this suggest about equipment properties?
items.answer: Acoustic testing can be replaced by random vibration tests for small compact space segment elements where acoustic testing does not provide adequate environmental simulation . This suggests that the equipment's size or structural properties are such that random vibrations more accurately replicate in-mission conditions compared to acoustic simulations .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: What role do functional and performance tests play before and after environmental test blocks, and why are they essential?
items.answer: Functional and performance tests before and after environmental test blocks help verify the space segment equipment's integrity and ensure no intermittent or persistent failures occur during environmental exposure . They are essential for confirming that the equipment remains operational and reliable within specified tolerances throughout testing phases .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: In what scenarios can electronic testing be limited to bonding tests, and what does this imply about the equipment's design complexity?
items.answer: Electronic testing can be limited to bonding tests in scenarios where the design complexity is minimal and the risk of contamination or failure is low, such as when the equipment's operational and mechanical integrity are assured through straightforward design . Limited testing implies a simplified testing model that can fully represent the function being tested without extensive testing beyond bonding, which indicates a less complex design .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: Why is thermal vacuum testing essential for space segment equipment, and what conditions must be met for these tests?
items.answer: Thermal vacuum testing is essential for space segment equipment to ensure its performance in the space vacuum environment, which the equipment will encounter at any point during its lifecycle . The test must be conducted at a pressure of 10-5 hPa or less to simulate the conditions of space accurately . Additionally, the test setup must prevent any contamination of the equipment, which involves starting with a maximum non-operating temperature , and the conditions must replicate the mission's cooling and heating speeds without exceeding them . Functional tests are necessary before and after the thermal test, and if the equipment includes heat pipes, they should be appropriately oriented (either horizontal or in reflux mode) during the test .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance

items.question: What types of space segment equipment require pressure tests, and what specific pressure-related conditions must these tests satisfy?
items.answer: Space segment equipment requiring pressure tests includes fluid or propulsion equipment, pressure vessels, and thrusters. These tests must satisfy conditions such as demonstrating conformity to specified maximum allowable leak rates, and they must be conducted before and after thermal and mechanical tests . For proof pressure tests, there should be no leaks or permanent deformations, and pressure cycling tests must consider temperature influences . These tests ensure reliability, aligning with expected operational environments and addressing safety and performance criteria .
items.title: ECSS-ST-10-03C Draft 12.5 | PDF | System | Resonance
